The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. {"code": "import json
import re

# Load the review data
review_path = var_functions.query_db:12
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load the books data
books_path = var_functions.query_db:14
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Print data sizes
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'num_reviews': len(reviews),
    'num_books': len(books),
    'sample_reviews': reviews[:3],
    'sample_books': books[:3]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the review data from the file path stored in the variable
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load the books data from the file path stored in the variable
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns (4 digits between 1800-2025)
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',  # 1900-2099
        r'\breleased on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\breleased in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\b(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December).*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
    ]
    
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {book['book_id']: book['decade'] for book in books_with_years}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample_books_with_years': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Simple function to extract year
def extract_year_simple(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Just look for any 4-digit number between 1800-2025
    pattern = r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)'
    match = re.search(pattern, details_text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Test with manual samples
sample_texts = [
    books[1].get('details', ''),  # bookid_2 with 1996
    books[2].get('details', ''),  # bookid_3 with 2012
    "The book, published by Soho Crime in a revised edition on July 1, 2003",
    books[14].get('details', ''), # bookid_15 with 2000
]

# Manually test with actual data
manual_tests = []
for i, book in enumerate(books[:15]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year_simple(details)
    manual_tests.append({
        'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
        'year': year,
        'details_preview': str(details)[:100]
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(manual_tests))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Just test regex directly with the actual strings
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Get the first string
sample_text = books[1].get('details', '')
print_raw_text = repr(sample_text)  # To see exactly what's in the string

# Test different patterns
test_patterns = [
    r'19\\d{2}',
    r'20\\d{2}',
    r'\\d{4}',
]

test_results = {}
for pattern_name, pattern in zip(['pattern1', 'pattern2', 'pattern3'], test_patterns):
    match = re.search(pattern, sample_text)
    test_results[pattern_name] = {
        'pattern': pattern,
        'found': bool(match),
        'match': match.group() if match else None
    }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'raw_text_preview': print_raw_text[:200],
    'test_results': test_results
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details - FIXED version
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns - proper regex for word boundaries
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            # Only accept years between 1800-2025
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Process all books AND DEBUG
books_with_years = []
for i, book in enumerate(books):
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })
    
    # Debug first few books
    if i < 5:
        print(f"Debug book {book_id}: year={year}, details={details[:80]}")

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:4 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

# Load the review data from the file path stored in the variable

# Print data sizes and samples

    'num_reviews': len(reviews),
    'num_books': len(books),
    'sample_reviews': reviews[:3],
    'sample_books': books[:3]
}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the review data from the file path stored in the variable
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load the books data from the file path stored in the variable
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns (4 digits between 1800-2025)
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',  # 1900-2099
        r'\breleased on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\breleased in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\b(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December).*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
    ]
    
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {book['book_id']: book['decade'] for book in books_with_years}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample_books_with_years': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Simple function to extract year
def extract_year_simple(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Just look for any 4-digit number between 1800-2025
    pattern = r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)'
    match = re.search(pattern, details_text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Test with manual samples
sample_texts = [
    books[1].get('details', ''),  # bookid_2 with 1996
    books[2].get('details', ''),  # bookid_3 with 2012
    "The book, published by Soho Crime in a revised edition on July 1, 2003",
    books[14].get('details', ''), # bookid_15 with 2000
]

# Manually test with actual data
manual_tests = []
for i, book in enumerate(books[:15]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year_simple(details)
    manual_tests.append({
        'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
        'year': year,
        'details_preview': str(details)[:100]
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(manual_tests))"}

    }

}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Load review data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Join reviews with book decades
# Note: review.purchase_id matches books.book_id
print(f"Total reviews: {len(reviews)}")
print(f"Books with year data: {len(book_to_decade)}")

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

no_match_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            if purchase_id in book_to_decade:
                decade = book_to_decade[purchase_id]
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                decade_books[decade].add(purchase_id)
            else:
                no_match_count += 1
        except ValueError:
            continue

print(f"Reviews without matching book: {no_match_count}")

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decades': decade_stats[:10],  # Top 10 by avg rating
    'all_decades': sorted([(d, len(decade_books[d]), sum(r)/len(r)) for d, r in decade_ratings.items()], 
                          key=lambda x: x[0])
}))"}

2. Join reviews with books using purchase_id/book_id
3. Calculate average ratings per decade
4. Filter only decades with at least 10 distinct books
5. Find the decade with the highest average rating

- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.

- execute_python: run Python code.

  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.

So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. - query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. {"code": "import json
import re

# Load the review data from the file path stored in the variable
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load the books data from the file path stored in the variable
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns (4 digits between 1800-2025)
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',  # 1900-2099
        r'\breleased on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\breleased in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\b(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December).*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
    ]
    
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {book['book_id']: book['decade'] for book in books_with_years}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample_books_with_years': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Simple function to extract year
def extract_year_simple(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Just look for any 4-digit number between 1800-2025
    pattern = r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)'
    match = re.search(pattern, details_text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Test with manual samples
sample_texts = [
    books[1].get('details', ''),  # bookid_2 with 1996
    books[2].get('details', ''),  # bookid_3 with 2012
    "The book, published by Soho Crime in a revised edition on July 1, 2003",
    books[14].get('details', ''), # bookid_15 with 2000
]

# Manually test with actual data
manual_tests = []
for i, book in enumerate(books[:15]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year_simple(details)
    manual_tests.append({
        'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
        'year': year,
        'details_preview': str(details)[:100]
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(manual_tests))"}

    }

}))"}

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:

    if matches:
        for match in matches:

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the review data from the file path stored in the variable
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load the books data from the file path stored in the variable
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns (4 digits between 1800-2025)
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',  # 1900-2099
        r'\breleased on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\breleased in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\b(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December).*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
    ]
    
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {book['book_id']: book['decade'] for book in books_with_years}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample_books_with_years': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Simple function to extract year
def extract_year_simple(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Just look for any 4-digit number between 1800-2025
    pattern = r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)'
    match = re.search(pattern, details_text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Test with manual samples
sample_texts = [
    books[1].get('details', ''),  # bookid_2 with 1996
    books[2].get('details', ''),  # bookid_3 with 2012
    "The book, published by Soho Crime in a revised edition on July 1, 2003",
    books[14].get('details', ''), # bookid_15 with 2000
]

# Manually test with actual data
manual_tests = []
for i, book in enumerate(books[:15]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year_simple(details)
    manual_tests.append({
        'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
        'year': year,
        'details_preview': str(details)[:100]
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(manual_tests))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Just test regex directly with the actual strings
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Get the first string
sample_text = books[1].get('details', '')
print_raw_text = repr(sample_text)  # To see exactly what's in the string

# Test different patterns
test_patterns = [
    r'19\\d{2}',
    r'20\\d{2}',
    r'\\d{4}',
]

test_results = {}
for pattern_name, pattern in zip(['pattern1', 'pattern2', 'pattern3'], test_patterns):
    match = re.search(pattern, sample_text)
    test_results[pattern_name] = {
        'pattern': pattern,
        'found': bool(match),
        'match': match.group() if match else None
    }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'raw_text_preview': print_raw_text[:200],
    'test_results': test_results
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details - FIXED version
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns (simpler approach)
    # Find all 4-digit numbers that could be years
    year_pattern = r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b'
    matches = re.findall(year_pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            # Only accept years between 1800-2025
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Process all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {book['book_id']: book['decade'] for book in books_with_years}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts.most_common(10)),  # Top 10 decades
    'sample_books_with_years': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details - FIXED version
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns - proper regex for word boundaries
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            # Only accept years between 1800-2025
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Process all books AND DEBUG
books_with_years = []
for i, book in enumerate(books):
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })
    
    # Debug first few books
    if i < 5:
        print(f"Debug book {book_id}: year={year}, details={details[:80]}")

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Load review data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Join reviews with book decades
# Note: review.purchase_id matches books.book_id
print(f"Total reviews: {len(reviews)}")
print(f"Books with year data: {len(book_to_decade)}")

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

no_match_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            if purchase_id in book_to_decade:
                decade = book_to_decade[purchase_id]
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                decade_books[decade].add(purchase_id)
            else:
                no_match_count += 1
        except ValueError:
            continue

print(f"Reviews without matching book: {no_match_count}")

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decades': decade_stats[:10],  # Top 10 by avg rating
    'all_decades': sorted([(d, len(decade_books[d]), sum(r)/len(r)) for d, r in decade_ratings.items()], 
                          key=lambda x: x[0])
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract numeric ids
review_ids = [r.get('purchase_id') for r in reviews if r.get('purchase_id')]
book_ids = [b.get('book_id') for b in books if b.get('book_id')]

# Check if any match
review_nums = set(r.split('_')[1] for r in review_ids if '_' in r)
book_nums = set(b.split('_')[1] for b in book_ids if '_' in b)
common = review_nums.intersection(book_nums)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_books': len(books),
    'sample_review_ids': review_ids[:5],
    'sample_book_ids': book_ids[:5],
    'common_numeric_ids_count': len(common),
    'sample_common_ids': list(common)[:10] if common else []
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the review data from the file path stored in the variable
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load the books data from the file path stored in the variable
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Look for year patterns (4 digits between 1800-2025)
    year_patterns = [
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',  # 1900-2099
        r'\breleased on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished on .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\breleased in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\bpublished in .*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
        r'\b(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December).*?\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b',
    ]
    
    for pattern in year_patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for all books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"  # Create decade string like "1990s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

# Count books per decade
from collections import Counter
decade_counts = Counter(book['decade'] for book in books_with_years)

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {book['book_id']: book['decade'] for book in books_with_years}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'decade_counts': dict(decade_counts),
    'sample_books_with_years': books_with_years[:10]
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Simple function to extract year
def extract_year_simple(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    # Just look for any 4-digit number between 1800-2025
    pattern = r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)'
    match = re.search(pattern, details_text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
            return year
    return None

# Test with manual samples
sample_texts = [
    books[1].get('details', ''),  # bookid_2 with 1996
    books[2].get('details', ''),  # bookid_3 with 2012
    "The book, published by Soho Crime in a revised edition on July 1, 2003",
    books[14].get('details', ''), # bookid_15 with 2000
]

# Manually test with actual data
manual_tests = []
for i, book in enumerate(books[:15]):
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year_simple(details)
    manual_tests.append({
        'book_id': book.get('book_id'),
        'year': year,
        'details_preview': str(details)[:100]
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(manual_tests))"}

    r'\\d{4}',
]

test_results = {}

    test_results[pattern_name] = {
        'pattern': pattern,
        'found': bool(match),
        'match': match.group() if match else None
    }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'raw_text_preview': print_raw_text[:200],
    'test_results': test_results
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Load review data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Join reviews with book decades
# Note: review.purchase_id matches books.book_id
print(f"Total reviews: {len(reviews)}")
print(f"Books with year data: {len(book_to_decade)}")

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

no_match_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            if purchase_id in book_to_decade:
                decade = book_to_decade[purchase_id]
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                decade_books[decade].add(purchase_id)
            else:
                no_match_count += 1
        except ValueError:
            continue

print(f"Reviews without matching book: {no_match_count}")

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decades': decade_stats[:10],  # Top 10 by avg rating
    'all_decades': sorted([(d, len(decade_books[d]), sum(r)/len(r)) for d, r in decade_ratings.items()], 
                          key=lambda x: x[0])
}))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
review_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(review_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:14']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year from book details
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text or not isinstance(details_text, str):
        return None
    
    pattern = r'(19\d{2}|20\d{2})'
    matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text)
    
    if matches:
        for match in matches:
            year = int(match)
            if 1800 <= year <= 2025:
                return year
    
    return None

# Create book_id to decade mapping (bookid_N format)
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book.get('book_id')
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Map review purchase_id (purchaseid_N) to decade by numeric ID
# Create mapping from numeric ID to decade based on book_to_decade
id_to_decade = {}
for book_id, decade in book_to_decade.items():
    # book_id is like "bookid_1"
    if '_' in book_id:
        num_id = book_id.split('_')[1]
        id_to_decade[num_id] = decade

# Group reviews by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(set)  # Set of book_ids per decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(list)  # List of ratings per decade

matched_reviews = 0
for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review.get('purchase_id')
    rating_str = review.get('rating')
    
    if purchase_id and rating_str and '_' in purchase_id:
        try:
            rating = float(rating_str)
            review_num_id = purchase_id.split('_')[1]
            
            if review_num_id in id_to_decade:
                decade = id_to_decade[review_num_id]
                # Add both the review id and book id to track unique books
                decade_books[decade].add(review_num_id)  # Use numeric ID to track unique books
                decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
                matched_reviews += 1
        except (ValueError, IndexError):
            continue

# Calculate average rating per decade with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, ratings in decade_ratings.items():
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'num_books': num_books,
            'num_reviews': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'matched_reviews': matched_reviews,
    'books_with_year_data': len(id_to_decade),
    'decades_with_10plus_books': len(decade_stats),
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None,
    'all_ranked_decades': decade_stats
}))"}
